author: Shafkat Farabi, Didac Marti Pinto, Wei Lu, Manuel Ramos-Maqueda, Sanmay Das, Antoine Deeb, Anja Sautmann

title: RA-QA: A Benchmarking System for Respiratory Audio Question Answering Under Real-World Heterogeneity
content:
arXiv:2602.18452v3 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: As conversational multimodal AI tools are increasingly adopted to process patient data for health assessment, robust benchmarks are needed to measure progress and expose failure modes under realistic conditions. Despite the importance of respiratory audio for mobile health screening, respiratory audio question answering remains underexplored, with existing studies evaluated narrowly and lacking real-world heterogeneity across modalities, devices, and question types. We hence introduce the \textbf{Respiratory-Audio Question-Answering (RA-QA) benchmark}, including a standardized data generation pipeline, a comprehensive multimodal QA collection, and a unified evaluation protocol. RA-QA harmonizes public RA datasets into a collection of 9 million format-diverse QA pairs covering diagnostic and contextual attributes. We benchmark general audio-language models as well as domain-specific architectures, establishing reproducible reference points and showing how current approaches fail under heterogeneity.
author: Gaia A. Bertolino, Yuwei Zhang, Tong Xia, Domenico Talia, Cecilia Mascolo

title: Enhancing Automatic Chord Recognition via Pseudo-Labeling and Knowledge Distillation
content:
arXiv:2602.19778v4 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Automatic Chord Recognition (ACR) is constrained by the scarcity of aligned chord labels, as well-aligned annotations are costly to acquire. At the same time, open-weight pre-trained models are more accessible than their proprietary training data. In this work, we present a two-stage training pipeline that leverages pre-trained models together with unlabeled audio. The proposed method decouples training into two stages. In the first stage, we use a pre-trained BTC model as a teacher to generate pseudo-labels for over 1,000 hours of diverse unlabeled audio and train a student model solely on these pseudo-labels. In the second stage, the student is continually trained on ground-truth labels as they become available. To prevent catastrophic forgetting of the representations learned in the first stage, we apply selective knowledge distillation (KD) from the teacher as a regularizer. In our experiments, two models (BTC, 2E1D) were used as students. In Stage 1, using only pseudo-labels, the BTC student achieves about 99% of the teacher's performance, while the 2E1D model achieves about 97% across seven standard mir_eval metrics. After a single training run for both students in Stage 2, the resulting BTC student model consistently surpasses both the traditional supervised learning baseline and the original pre-trained teacher model across all metrics. The resulting 2E1D student model also outperforms the supervised baseline and approaches teacher-level performance, with both models demonstrating significant gains on rare chord qualities.
author: Nghia Phan, Rong Jin, Gang Liu, Xiao Dong

title: Inference-time optimization for experiment-grounded protein ensemble generation
content:
arXiv:2602.24007v3 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Protein function relies on dynamic conformational ensembles, yet current generative models like AlphaFold3 often fail to produce ensembles that match experimental data. Recent experiment-guided generators attempt to address this by steering the reverse diffusion process. However, these methods are limited by fixed sampling horizons and sensitivity to initialization, often yielding thermodynamically implausible results. We introduce a general inference-time optimization framework to solve these challenges. First, we optimize over latent representations to maximize ensemble log-likelihood, rather than perturbing structures post hoc. This approach eliminates dependence on diffusion length, removes initialization bias, and easily incorporates external constraints. Second, we present novel sampling schemes for drawing Boltzmann-weighted ensembles. By combining structural priors from AlphaFold3 with force-field-based priors, we sample from their product distribution while balancing experimental likelihoods. Our results show that this framework consistently outperforms state-of-the-art guidance, improving diversity, physical energy, and agreement with data in X-ray crystallography and NMR, often fitting the experimental data better than deposited PDB structures. Finally, inference-time optimization experiments maximizing ipTM scores reveal that perturbing AlphaFold3 embeddings can artificially inflate model confidence. This exposes a vulnerability in current design metrics, whose mitigation could offer a pathway to reduce false discovery rates in binder engineering.
author: Advaith Maddipatla, Anar Rzayev, Marco Pegoraro, Martin Pacesa, Paul Schanda, Ailie Marx, Sanketh Vedula, Alex M. Bronstein

title: The Hidden Cost of Structured Generation in LLMs: Draft-Conditioned Constrained Decoding
content:
arXiv:2603.03305v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate executable outputs, JSON objects, and API calls, where a single syntax error can make the output unusable. Constrained decoding enforces validity token-by-token via masking and renormalization, but it can distort generation when the model assigns low probability mass to valid continuations, pushing decoding toward locally valid yet semantically incorrect trajectories. We propose \emph{Draft-Conditioned Constrained Decoding (DCCD)}, a simple two-step, training-free inference procedure that decouples semantic planning from structural enforcement: an unconstrained draft is generated first, and constrained decoding is then applied, conditioned on this draft, to guarantee validity. We analyze DCCD through a KL-projection view, showing that draft conditioning increases feasible mass and reduces the cumulative "projection tax" induced by hard constraints, with an optional best-of-$K$ draft selection. Across structured reasoning benchmarks, DCCD improves strict structured accuracy by up to +24 percentage points over standard constrained decoding (e.g., 15.2\% to 39.0\% on GSM8K with a 1B model), and enables smaller model pairs to match or exceed much larger constrained baselines, yielding substantial gains in parameter efficiency.
author: Avinash Reddy, Thayne T. Walker, James S. Ide, Amrit Singh Bedi

title: HiAP: A Multi-Granular Stochastic Auto-Pruning Framework for Vision Transformers
content:
arXiv:2603.12222v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Vision Transformers require significant computational resources and memory bandwidth, severely limiting their deployment on resource-constraint hardware. Most structured pruning methods reduce theoretical cost effectively, yet they typically operate at a single structural granularity and depend on multi-stage pipelines with importance ranking, auxiliary solvers or post-hoc magnitude thresholding, followed by a separate fine-tuning phase to recover accuracy. We propose Hierarchical Auto-Pruning (HiAP), which casts ViT pruning as a single budget-aware learning problem and jointly allocates sparsity across four granularities in one end-to-end phase. HiAP introduces stochastic Gumbel-Sigmoid gates at macro level (attention heads and FFN blocks) and micro level (intra-head dimensions and FFN neurons), and optimizes them against the task loss together with an analytical MAC cost term. The budget coefficient steers the network to a target compute level while the gates gradually harden into a dense, smaller sub-network at convergence. It does not require importance heuristics, ranking metrics, auxillary solvers or secondary fine-tuning. On ImageNet with DeiT small, HiAP automatically discovers hetergenous architectures, pruning depths, heads, and width by different amount across layers, and reaches competitive accuracies against substantially more complex pruning pipelines at comparable compute from a single training run.
author: Andy Li, Aiden Durrant, Milan Markovic, Georgios Leontidis

title: Spatio-temporal probabilistic forecast using MMAF-guided learning
content:
arXiv:2603.15055v3 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: We present a theory-guided generalized Bayesian methodology for spatio-temporal raster data, which we use to train an ensemble of stochastic feed-forward neural networks with Gaussian-distributed weights. The methodology incorporates the dependence and causal structure of a spatio-temporal Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process into training and inference by enforcing constraints on the design of the data embedding and the related optimization routine. In inference mode, the networks are employed to generate causal ensemble forecasts by applying different initial conditions at different horizons. We call this workflow MMAF-guided learning. Experiments conducted on both synthetic and real data demonstrate that our forecasts remain calibrated across multiple time horizons. Moreover, we show that on such data, shallow feed-forward architectures can achieve performance comparable to, and in some cases better than, convolutional or diffusion deep learning architectures used in probabilistic forecasting tasks.
author: Leonardo Bardi, Imma Valentina Curato, Lorenzo Proietti

title: How Alignment Routes: Localizing, Scaling, and Controlling Policy Circuits in Language Models
content:
arXiv:2604.04385v5 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: We localize the policy routing mechanism in alignment-trained language models. An intermediate-layer attention gate reads detected content and triggers deeper amplifier heads that boost the signal toward refusal. In smaller models the gate and amplifier are single heads; at larger scale they become bands of heads across adjacent layers. The gate contributes under 1% of output DLA, yet interchange testing (p < 0.001) and knockout cascade confirm it is causally necessary. Interchange screening at n >= 120 detects the same motif in twelve models from six labs (2B to 72B), though specific heads differ by lab. Per-head ablation weakens up to 58x at 72B and misses gates that interchange identifies; at scale, interchange is the only reliable audit. Modulating the detection-layer signal continuously controls policy from hard refusal through evasion to factual answering. On safety prompts the same intervention turns refusal into harmful guidance, showing that the safety-trained capability is gated by routing, not removed. Thresholds vary by topic and by input language, and the circuit relocates across generations within a family even while behavioral benchmarks register no change. Routing is early-commitment: the gate fires at its own layer before deeper layers finish processing the input. An in-context substitution cipher collapses gate interchange necessity by 70 to 99% across three models, and the model switches to puzzle-solving rather than refusal. Injecting the plaintext gate activation into the cipher forward pass restores 48% of refusals in Phi-4-mini, localizing the bypass to the routing interface. A second method, cipher contrast analysis, uses plain/cipher DLA differences to map the full cipher-sensitive routing circuit in O(3n) forward passes. Any encoding that defeats detection-layer pattern matching bypasses the policy regardless of whether deeper layers reconstruct the content.
author: Gregory N. Frank

title: Symbiotic-MoE: Unlocking the Synergy between Generation and Understanding
content:
arXiv:2604.07753v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Empowering Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) with image generation often leads to catastrophic forgetting in understanding tasks due to severe gradient conflicts. While existing paradigms like Mixture-of-Transformers (MoT) mitigate this conflict through structural isolation, they fundamentally sever cross-modal synergy and suffer from capacity fragmentation. In this work, we present Symbiotic-MoE, a unified pre-training framework that resolves task interference within a native multimodal Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) Transformers architecture with zero-parameter overhead. We first identify that standard MoE tuning leads to routing collapse, where generative gradients dominate expert utilization. To address this, we introduce Modality-Aware Expert Disentanglement, which partitions experts into task-specific groups while utilizing shared experts as a multimodal semantic bridge. Crucially, this design allows shared experts to absorb fine-grained visual semantics from generative tasks to enrich textual representations. To optimize this, we propose a Progressive Training Strategy featuring differential learning rates and early-stage gradient shielding. This mechanism not only shields pre-trained knowledge from early volatility but eventually transforms generative signals into constructive feedback for understanding. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Symbiotic-MoE achieves rapid generative convergence while unlocking cross-modal synergy, boosting inherent understanding with remarkable gains on MMLU and OCRBench.
author: Xiangyue Liu, Zijian Zhang, Miles Yang, Zhao Zhong, Liefeng Bo, Ping Tan

title: A Synonymous Variational Perspective on the Rate-Distortion-Perception Tradeoff
content:
arXiv:2604.14603v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: The fundamental limit of natural signal compression has traditionally been characterized by classical rate-distortion (RD) theory through the tradeoff between coding rate and reconstruction distortion, while the rate-distortion-perception (RDP) framework introduces a divergence-based measure of perceptual quality as a modeling principle, leaving its theoretical origin unclear. In this paper, motivated by a synonymity-based semantic information perspective, we reformulate perceptual reconstruction as recovering any admissible sample within an ideal synonymous set (synset) associated with the source, rather than the source sample itself, and establish a synonymous source coding architecture. On this basis, we develop a synonymous variational inference (SVI) analysis framework with a synonymous variational lower bound (SVLBO) for tractable analysis of synset-oriented compression. Within this framework, we establish a synonymity-perception consistency principle, showing that optimal identification of semantic information is theoretically consistent with perceptual optimization. Based on this result, we further derive a tight-bound synonymous source coding rate characterization and show that its Jensen-limit relaxation leads to a synonymous rate-distortion-perception form for practical optimization. These analytical results show that the distributional divergence term arises naturally from the synset-based reconstruction objective, clarify its compatibility with existing RDP formulations and classical RD theory, and suggest the potential advantages of synonymous source coding.
author: Zijian Liang, Kai Niu, Changshuo Wang, Jin Xu, Ping Zhang

title: Identifiability and Stability of Generative Drifting with Companion-Elliptic Kernel Families
content:
arXiv:2604.24196v3 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: This paper studies the identifiability and stability of drifting fields in the framework of Generative Modeling via Drifting. The motivating question is whether a zero-drift equilibrium identifies the target distribution and whether an approximately vanishing drift implies weak distributional convergence. Since the original drifting model employs the Laplace kernel by default, we first analyze why Gaussian score-based arguments fail to apply. This analysis motivates the introduction of companion-elliptic kernel families, which are characterized by a companion potential satisfying an elliptic closure relation. We show that this class naturally contains the Laplace kernel and consists precisely of Gaussian and Mat\'ern kernels with smoothness parameter $\nu>0$. Within this class, we establish field identifiability for arbitrary Borel probability measures on $R^d$: if the drifting field between two such measures vanishes identically, then they must coincide. For stability, we demonstrate that convergence of the field alone does not guarantee weak convergence, since mass may escape to infinity while remaining invisible to the field. Although tightness directly removes this obstruction and restores weak stability, we prove that, even without tightness, every $C_0$-vague cluster point lies exactly on the defect ray $\{cp:0\le c\le1\}$. Consequently, a single scalar $C_0$ observable suffices to detect the missing mass and recover weak convergence.
author: HakGeun Lee, Hyonho Chun

title: Distributional Causal Mediation via Conditional Generative Modeling
content:
arXiv:2605.01765v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Mediation analysis has traditionally focused on outcome-level summary contrasts, such as mean effects, which may obscure substantial distributional changes induced by complex and nonlinear causal mechanisms. We propose Distributional Causal Mediation Analysis (DCMA), a generative learning framework for identifying and estimating treatment effects on entire outcome distributions transmitted through multiple mediators. DCMA learns conditional generative models for the mediators and the outcome, recovering the relevant conditional distributions from observational data. Leveraging the identification formulas, it reconstructs interventional outcome distributions via Monte Carlo forward simulation by noise resampling, enabling the capture of both classical summary effects and rich distributional contrasts such as energy distance and the Wasserstein distance. Analytical error bounds are derived to decompose how estimation errors in the learned conditional models propagate to the reconstructed interventional outcome distributions. The empirical effectiveness of DCMA is demonstrated through numerical experiments and real-world data applications.
author: Jinlun Zhang, Haoneng Huang, Zishu Zhan, Chunquan Ou

title: On the Spectral Structure and Objective Equivalence of Orthogonal Multilabel Fisher Discriminants
content:
arXiv:2605.03283v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: We provide a unified theoretical analysis of Linear Discriminant Analysis with simultaneous multilabel scatter matrix formulations and Stiefel orthogonality constraints. Our contributions span both algebraic structure and statistical guarantees. On the algebraic side, we characterize the rank of the multilabel between-class scatter matrix, showing that the effective discriminant dimensionality can strictly exceed the classical single-label bound of $C-1$; we establish a multilabel partition of variance and prove that all four Fisher objectives are equivalent under the $W^\top S_t^{ML} W = I_r$ constraint while characterizing their divergence under the Stiefel constraint; and we prove a two-sided label-distance preservation bound relating projected distances to Hamming distances in label space. On the statistical side, we establish a finite-sample $O(k_{\max}\sqrt{d\log d/n}/gap_r)$ bound on the subspace estimation error under sub-Gaussian noise with a matching $\Omega(\sigma^2 d/(n\,gap_r))$ minimax lower bound, establishing a near-minimax-optimal rate (matching up to logarithmic and $k_{\max}$ factors) for multilabel discriminant subspace estimation. We further provide high-probability distance concentration, robustness guarantees under label interactions, and a regularization analysis preserving the spectral structure when $d \gg n$. All results are verified numerically on synthetic data generated from the linear label-effect model, covering both the algebraic identities and the multilabel-specific quantities ($k_{\max}$, $\kappa(S_t^{ML})$, $\|\Gamma/n\|_2$, $\Delta_r$) that govern the statistical bounds. The numerical experiments are designed as a sanity check for the theorems rather than as an empirical benchmark; evaluation on real multilabel datasets is left to future work targeting application-oriented venues.
author: Brian Keith-Norambuena, Juan Bekios-Calfa

title: Globally Optimal Training of Spiking Neural Networks via Parameter Reconstruction
content:
arXiv:2605.08022v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) have been proposed as biologically plausible and energy-efficient alternatives to conventional Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs). However, the training of SNN usually relies on surrogate gradients due to the non-differentiability of the spike function, introducing approximation errors that accumulate across layers. To address this challenge, we extend the work on convexification of parallel feedforward threshold networks to parallel recurrent threshold networks, which subsume parallel SNNs as a structured special case. Building on this theoretical framework, we propose a parameter reconstruction algorithm for SNN training that demonstrates consistent and significant advantages across various tasks, both as a standalone method and in combination with surrogate-gradient training. The ablations further demonstrate the data scalability and robustness to model configurations of our training algorithm, pointing toward its potential in large-scale SNN training.
author: Himanshu Udupi, Xiaocong Yang, ChengXiang Zhai

title: Robust Multi-Agent LLMs under Byzantine Faults
content:
arXiv:2605.09076v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents increasingly collaborate over peer-to-peer networks to improve their reliability. However, these same interactions can also become a source of vulnerability, as unreliable or Byzantine agents may sway neighboring agents toward incorrect conclusions and degrade overall system performance. Existing methods rely on leader-based coordination or self-reported confidence, both of which are susceptible to adversarial manipulation. We study decentralized LLM multi-agent systems (LLM-MAS) and propose Self-Anchored Consensus (SAC), a fully decentralized iterative filter-and-refine protocol in which agents iteratively exchange responses, locally evaluate and filter unreliable messages, and refine their own outputs. We present $(F{+}1)$-robustness conditions for the communication graph that ensure honest agents preserve and propagate reliable information despite Byzantine influence. Experiments on mathematical and commonsense reasoning benchmarks show that SAC effectively suppresses Byzantine influence and consistently improves performance across diverse communication topologies, whereas prior methods degrade under adversarial conditions.
author: Haejoon Lee, Vincent-Daniel Yun, Dimitra Panagou, Sai Praneeth Karimireddy

title: Optimal Regret for Single Index Bandits
content:
arXiv:2605.09454v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: We study the $\textit{single-index bandit}$ problem, where rewards depend on an unknown one-dimensional projection of high-dimensional contexts through an unknown reward function. This model extends linear and generalized linear bandits to a nonparametric setting, and is particularly relevant when the reward function is not known in advance. While optimal regret guarantees are known for monotone reward functions, the general non-monotone case remains poorly understood, with the best known bound being $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(T^{3/4})$ (under standard boundedness and Lipschitz assumptions on the reward function [Kang et al., 2025]).
  We close this gap by establishing the optimal regret for general single-index bandits. We propose a simple two-phase algorithm, namely, Zoomed Single Index Bandit with Upper Confidence Bound ($\texttt{ZoomSIB-UCB}$), that first estimates the projection direction via a normalized Stein estimator, and then reduces the problem to a one-dimensional bandit using discretization and finally use UCB. This approach achieves a regret of $\tilde{\mathcal{O}}(T^{2/3})$, and improves significantly upon prior work without any additional assumptions. We also prove a matching minimax lower bound of $\tilde{\Omega}(T^{2/3})$, showing that the upper bound is essentially tight. Our upper and lower bounds together provide a sharp characterization of the regret in single-index bandits. Moreover, the empirical results further demonstrate the effectiveness and robustness of our approach.
author: Devdan Dey, Sujoy Bhore, Avishek Ghosh

title: R-DMesh: Video-Guided 3D Animation via Rectified Dynamic Mesh Flow
content:
arXiv:2605.13838v3 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Video-guided 3D animation holds immense potential for content creation, offering intuitive and precise control over dynamic assets. However, practical deployment faces a critical yet frequently overlooked hurdle: the pose misalignment dilemma. In real-world scenarios, the initial pose of a user-provided static mesh rarely aligns with the starting frame of a reference video. Naively forcing a mesh to follow a mismatched trajectory inevitably leads to severe geometric distortion or animation failure. To address this, we present Rectified Dynamic Mesh (R-DMesh), a unified framework designed to generate high-fidelity 4D meshes that are ``rectified'' to align with video context. Unlike standard motion transfer approaches, our method introduces a novel VAE that explicitly disentangles the input into a conditional base mesh, relative motion trajectories, and a crucial rectification jump offset. This offset is learned to automatically transform the arbitrary pose of the input mesh to match the video's initial state before animation begins. We process these components via a Triflow Attention mechanism, which leverages vertex-wise geometric features to modulate the three orthogonal flows, ensuring physical consistency and local rigidity during the rectification and animation process. For generation, we employ a Rectified Flow-based Diffusion Transformer conditioned on pre-trained video latents, effectively transferring rich spatio-temporal priors to the 3D domain. To support this task, we construct Video-RDMesh, a large-scale dataset of over 500k dynamic mesh sequences specifically curated to simulate pose misalignment. Extensive experiments demonstrate that R-DMesh not only solves the alignment problem but also enables robust downstream applications, including pose retargeting and holistic 4D generation.
author: Zijie Wu, Lixin Xu, Puhua Jiang, Sicong Liu, Chunchao Guo, Xiang Bai

title: Road Maps as Free Geometric Priors: Weather-Invariant Drone Geo-Localization with GeoFuse
content:
arXiv:2605.14925v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Drone-view geo-localization aims to match a query drone image, often captured under adverse weather conditions (e.g., rain, snow, fog), against a gallery of geo-tagged satellite images. Weather-induced degradations in the drone view, such as noise, reduced visibility, and partial occlusions, severely exacerbate the intrinsic cross-view domain gap. While prior methods predominantly rely on weather-specific architectures or data augmentations, they have largely overlooked road map data, a readily available modality that provides strong, inherently weather-invariant geometric layout cues (e.g., road networks and building footprints) at negligible additional cost. We introduce GeoFuse, a cross-modal fusion framework that integrates precisely aligned road map tiles with satellite imagery to yield more discriminative and weather-resilient representations. We first augment the existing University-1652 and DenseUAV benchmarks with geo-aligned road maps, supplying structural priors robust to meteorological variations. Building on this, we propose a flexible fusion module that combines satellite and road map features via token-level and channel-level interactions, with a lightweight dynamic gating mechanism that adaptively weights modality contributions per instance. Finally, we employ class-level cross-view contrastive learning to promote robust alignment between weather-degraded drone features and the fused satellite-roadmap representations. Extensive experiments under diverse weather conditions show that GeoFuse consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods, achieving +3.46% and +23.18% Recall@1 accuracy on the University-1652 and DenseUAV benchmarks, respectively.
author: Yunsong Fang, Tingyu Wang, Zhedong Zheng

title: CADS: Conformal Adaptive Decision System for Cost-Efficient Image Classification
content:
arXiv:2605.16401v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: While high-capacity AI models have advanced state-of-the-art performance, their practical deployment is often hindered by high inference costs, environmental impact, and a "one-size-fits-all" approach that ignores varying sample complexity. In clinical settings for instance, the waste of computational resources on routine cases is a significant barrier to sustainable AI. In this paper, we introduce the Conformal Adaptive Decision System (CADS), a sequential multi-model algorithm designed to optimize resource allocation by efficiently sampling models based on the estimated data complexity. CADS leverages conformal prediction to quantify image uncertainty at runtime. CADS provides a mathematically grounded framework for balancing the cost-accuracy dilemma that dynamically routes samples through a model cascade, ranging from lightweight "Scout" models to high-capacity "Oracle" architectures. Validated on two datasets, CADS demonstrated superior efficiency and accuracy at a computational cost that can be up to 12 times lower than heavy-model inference. By accurately routing samples based on real-time complexity, CADS ensures high diagnostic reliability while drastically reducing the economic and environmental footprint of AI.
author: Mikael Turkoglu, Tim Bary, Vincent Thielens, Manon Dausort, Beno\^it Macq

title: Diffusion Models Observe Only Gradients: A Geometric Perspective on Score Matching Errors
content:
arXiv:2606.06179v2 Announce Type: replace-cross 
Abstract: Score-based diffusion models are typically trained by minimizing the $L^2$ score matching error, and standard theoretical analyses rely on this quantity to bound the sampling discrepancy between the learned and target distributions. We show the $L^2$ score error is not the right intrinsic measure of marginal distributional quality: a learned diffusion model can incur arbitrarily large $L^2$ score error while perfectly matching the target distribution. By decomposing score errors into a gradient and a solenoidal component (a Helmholtz-Hodge decomposition), we identify the geometric reason behind this: only the gradient component enters the marginal Fokker-Planck dynamics, while the solenoidal component is structurally invisible. We make this precise in three results. First, building on the corrected geometry, we prove an impossibility result: no monotone function of the $L^2$ score error can uniformly lower bound any divergence between the learned and target distributions. Second, we derive an upper bound on the Kullback-Leibler divergence that depends only on the observable gradient component of the error, tightening the standard Girsanov bound for generic score networks, and identifying its looseness as the cost of operating on path-space rather than marginal-space dynamics. Third, we give a tractable estimator of the gradient component via a dual Sobolev identity, which is shown to empirically correlate substantially better with sample quality than the full $L^2$ error.
author: Na\"il B. Khelifa, Richard E. Turner, Ramji Venkataramanan

title: Single Channel Receiver Architecture for Auto-Calibrated Passive Reflectometry Using Wideband Signals
content: A compact single-channel receiver architecture for passive reflectometry using signals of opportunity (SoOP) is presented. It aims to provide a simple receiver architecture that can be employed for surface and root-zone soil moisture (RZSM) measurements. The main characteristic of this architecture is that direct and reflected signals are collected with a single antenna and down-conversion chain, and that this has the advantage that the system is auto-calibrated, as direct and reflected signals are affected identically by the instrument (antenna pattern needs to be characterized). The use of a single antenna and receiver channels allows for reducing the mass and power consumption, which is especially important for small-format platforms such as drones and nano-satellites. In addition, the processing method provides means to reduce the effect of the receiver noise on the measurements. This article presents the instrumental concept, simulation results, and a laboratory verification of the proposed architecture and methods.
author: Serni Ribó, Xavier Canalda, Estel Cardellach, Weiqiang Li, Antonio Rius

title: Reconstruction of TROPOMI NO2 and HCHO Vertical Column Densities Over the Fenwei Plain Based on Spatiotemporal Tensor Completion
content: The vertical column densities (VCDs) of total nitrogen dioxide (NO2) and tropospheric formaldehyde (HCHO) in the atmosphere serve as core foundational elements for regional environmental planning management, air quality monitoring and control, and atmospheric environmental mechanism research. While satellite remote sensing technology enables long-term, large-scale observations, factors such as cloud and rain obscuration result in extensive data gaps ranging from 15% to 60%, severely limiting the analysis of the spatiotemporal patterns of pollution. To address the spatial data gaps in the VCD products of NO2 and HCHO using satellite data from the tropospheric monitoring instrument (TROPOMI), this article focuses on the Fenwei Plain in China and proposes a reconstruction method for atmospheric remote sensing products based on spatiotemporal adaptive block tensor completion. By mining the spatiotemporal neighborhood similarities and interannual periodic characteristics of pollutant VCDs, this article constructs a three-dimensional (3-D) spatiotemporal tensor with low-rank characteristics. It further introduces an adaptive mechanism that dynamically allocates weights based on the low-rank property of each dimension to achieve seamless spatiotemporal reconstruction of TROPOMI NO2 and HCHO VCDs over the Fenwei Plain from 2019 to 2023. The results demonstrate that this approach elevates the spatiotemporal coverage of both atmospheric pollutant VCD datasets to 100%. Even when the NO2 data gap reaches 50%, a high reconstruction accuracy is maintained (the coefficient of determination reaches 0.90). When the HCHO data gap reaches 30%, the reconstruction accuracy is also satisfactory (the coefficient of determination reaches 0.83) while improving the computational efficiency by 69%–84%. This method not only meets the trace gas data needs for regional pollution prevention and control but also provides a technical paradigm for remote sensing data reconstruction, with significant strategic and methodological value.
author: Jiajia Chen, Ziwei Li, Dong Chu, Linkan Li, Bohan Xu, Shenghao Bai, Weiguo Wang

title: Improving Tropical Cyclone Intensity Forecast Through Uncertainty Estimation and Long-Term Priors
content: In recent years, deep learning (DL) has made considerable progress in tropical cyclone (TC) intensity forecast. However, the existing DL models are constrained by their deterministic nature and a lack of modeling for long-term evolution patterns, thus precluding uncertainty estimation and limiting their generalization on complicated intensity evolution. To overcome these deficiencies, we propose a DL model by incorporating uncertainty estimation and long-term priors. Through learning distributional representations to jointly estimate data and output uncertainty and encoding historical patterns into graph convolution layers as a prior, our model enhances its 24-h forecasting performance, showing promising capability in handling complex intensity fluctuations. To evaluate the performance of our proposed model, we collect TC data from 2000 to 2022 and compare it with several state-of-the-art DL and operational models. Experimental results show that our model can reduce the forecasting errors by 14.35% and 9.91% in comparison with the best-performing operational model China Meteorological Administration (CMA) and the leading DL baseline (VQLTI), respectively. The code of our proposed model will be released at https://github.com/captain-slow-tc/tcip-lpue
author: Zihao Xu, Renlong Hang, Jiuwei Zhao, Gaozhen Nie, Qingshan Liu

title: RS-MTDF: Multiteacher Distillation and Fusion for Remote Sensing Semi-Supervised Semantic Segmentation
content: Semantic segmentation in remote sensing (RS) images is crucial for various applications, yet its performance is heavily reliant on large-scale, high-quality pixel-wise annotations, which are notoriously expensive and time-consuming to acquire. Semi-supervised semantic segmentation (SSS) offers a promising alternative to mitigate this data dependency. However, existing SSS methods often struggle with the inherent distribution mismatch between limited labeled data and abundant unlabeled data, leading to suboptimal generalization. To alleviate this issue, we attempt to introduce vision foundation models (VFMs) pretrained on vast and diverse datasets into the SSS task since VFMs possess robust generalization capabilities that can effectively bridge this distribution gap and provide strong semantic priors for SSS. Inspired by this, we introduce RS-MTDF (multiteacher distillation and fusion), a novel framework that leverages the powerful semantic knowledge embedded in VFMs to guide semi-supervised learning in RS. Specifically, RS-MTDF employs multiple frozen VFMs (e.g., DINOv2 and CLIP) as expert teachers, utilizing feature-level distillation to align student features with their robust representations. To further enhance discriminative power, the distilled knowledge is seamlessly fused into the student decoder. Extensive experiments on three challenging RS datasets (ISPRS Potsdam, LoveDA, and DeepGlobe) demonstrate that RS-MTDF achieves state-of-the-art or comparable performance. Notably, our method outperforms existing approaches across various label ratios on LoveDA and secures the highest IoU in the majority of semantic categories. These results underscore the efficacy of multiteacher VFM guidance in significantly enhancing both generalization and semantic understanding for RS segmentation. Ablation studies further validate the contribution of each proposed module. Code is available at https://github.com/earth-insights/RS-MTDF
author: Jiayi Song, Kaiyu Li, Jing Yao, Xiangyong Cao, Deyu Meng

title: Physically Driven Source-Aware Decoupling and Degradation-Aware Diffusion for Infrared Image Enhancement
content: Infrared imaging for remote sensing follows a complex physical pipeline that introduces multiple coupled degradations, making its robust enhancement fundamentally distinct from conventional RGB image restoration. Most existing infrared enhancement methods target a single degradation type, and their extension to joint multidegradation handling often suffers from severe error accumulation and mutual interference. To address this challenge, we propose source-aware degradation decoupling diffusion framework for infrared image enhancement (SDDiff-IR), a novel physics-driven end-to-end infrared enhancement pipeline for joint multisource degradation removal. Motivated by the infrared imaging physical process, SDDiff-IR first uses the physically inspired decomposition network to decompose degraded infrared images into two physically interpretable components: a radiation map and a detail map, corresponding to scene-to-detector and detector-to-signal degradations, respectively. The radiation map mainly captures low-frequency radiation bias caused by scene radiation, atmospheric effects, and the optical system. To correct the radiation bias, we design a generative diffusion model to reconstruct the underlying radiation distribution. On the contrary, the detail map characterizes high-frequency degradations originating from the infrared focal plane array (FPA) and readout circuitry, including stripe and Gaussian noise. We introduce a degradation-aware guided diffusion model that jointly learns degradation synthesis and restoration, enabling effective guidance of the diffusion process for modeling and removing multiple noise sources. Extensive experiments on public real-world infrared datasets demonstrate that SDDiff-IR consistently outperforms state-of-the-art infrared enhancement methods in both quantitative and qualitative evaluations.
author: Xinyue Su, Sitian Liu, Haoze Song, Liheng Bian

title: Physics-Guided Adaptive Regime Learning for Nationwide X-Band Dual-Polarization Radar Attenuation Correction
content: X-band dual-polarization radars support high-resolution monitoring of severe convection, yet their operational value is fundamentally limited by rain attenuation that distorts reflectivity and polarimetric signatures. We develop a nationwide physics-guided adaptive regime-learning framework for X-band attenuation correction using strict X–S matched supervision. The framework is trained on 44.874 million quality-controlled X–S matched samples spanning seven major climate regions of China. It anchors correction to  $\Phi _{DP}$ , introduces  $Z_{DR}$ -conditioned regime partitioning to represent microphysical heterogeneity, and adopts region–season adaptive parameterization for nationwide deployment. Benchmark tests at representative sites in Beijing and Jiangsu show that the six-regime hybrid model outperforms both the conventional baseline and the supervised benchmark. For  $Z_{H}$ , RMSE/MAE are reduced by 12.2%/14.5% in Beijing and 14.7%/18.2% in Jiangsu relative to the conventional differential-phase (DP) baseline. For  $Z_{DR}$ , the conventional baseline actively degrades correction; the proposed framework achieves RMSE of 0.65/0.35 dB and MAE of 0.43/0.26 dB with MBIAS near zero. Regional evaluations cover six representative sites across five climate regions, including a semi-arid Northwest China boundary case where the conventional baseline fails catastrophically. MAE reductions reach 52.5%–62.6% across five sites, while the sixth maintains a stable corrected MAE of 3.40 dB. Independent severe-convective cases demonstrate stable storm-core recovery with attenuation-induced bias reduced to near zero and RMSE/MAE decreases exceeding 58%. These results show that nationwide crossband supervision and physics-guided adaptive regime learning provide a scalable solution for dense X-band network deployment in support of quantitative precipitation estimation (QPE) and short-fuse warning.
author: Lei Wu, Lin Zhang, Qiqi Yang, Feng Li, Chenxi Zhou, Ruiyi Li, Yangming Wang, Yibo Wu

title: An Adaptive Scaling-Based Pauli Color-Coding for Polarimetric SAR Images
content: Polarimetric synthetic aperture radar (PolSAR) images are complex fourth-order tensors, each pixel corresponding to a Hermitian matrix with the comprehensive target scattering information. To visualize this hypermatrix data for target recognition, the Pauli color coding (PC) is widely used to decompose the scattering matrix into three Pauli components and map the coherency matrix diagonal elements  ${T}_{ll}$  ( ${l}={1, 2,~\mathrm {and}~3}$ ) to red–green–blue (RGB) channels for color image synthesis. Despite its simplicity and efficiency, PC has two critical limitations: fixed coherent scattering models fail to adaptively capture the dielectric and geometric properties of surface/double-bounce scattering or depolarization effects inherent to volume scattering, and  ${T}_{ll}$ ’s large dynamic range renders it unsuitable for direct use as a color component. To address these challenges, an adaptive PC (APC) scheme is proposed. It first linearly scales  ${T}_{ll}$  with a scaling factor  $s$ , followed by nested sequential normalization on the scaled components to ensure distinct scaling effects for  ${T}_{11}$ ,  ${T}_{22}$ , and  ${T}_{33}$ . By simply adjusting  $s$ , the scheme can operate three normalization modes: increscent, decrescent, and routine. The increscent scaling is distinctive in that it shares highly similar logic with model-based color coding (MC), which enhances the weight of volume scattering while adaptively improving surface/double-bounce scattering contributions. Thus, APC realizes color coding of scattering characteristics highly consistent with MC by relying solely on  ${T}_{ll}$ . It not only avoids negative- and zero-value color rendering caused by the overestimation of volume scattering inherent to MC but also precludes color saturation or darkening induced by the large dynamic range of scattering power, while retaining high efficiency. Qualitative and quantitative comparisons of APC versus typical PC and MC methods conducted on C-/L-/P-band spaceborne and airborne datasets validate its excellent performance in visualization quality, scattering mechanism preservation, positive color rendering, computational efficiency, and robustness to urban façade orientations.
author: Tianwei Liu, Dong Li, Yunhua Zhang, Xun Wang

title: HOTMM: Direction-Aware Spatial–Spectral Mamba With Long-Range Memory Guidance for Hyperspectral Object Tracking
content: Hyperspectral object tracking (HOT) plays an increasingly important role in remote sensing surveillance, as rich spectral signatures in hyperspectral (HS) imagery enable more reliable target discrimination. However, existing HOT methods still face challenges in fully exploiting spatial–spectral–temporal interactions, including limited spatial adaptivity, insufficient spectral integration, and inadequate temporal context modeling. Although recent Mamba-based trackers offer effective sequence modeling for HOT, their predefined scanning paths with uniform aggregation tend to overlook the diverse directional structures in remote sensing scenarios. To address these challenges, this article proposes HOTMM, a novel Mamba-based framework that unifies direction-aware spatial–spectral modeling with long-range memory-guided temporal modulation to jointly capture directional spatial structures, interband spectral dependencies, and persistent target evolution. At the core of the framework is the mixture of directional Mamba experts (MDMEs) module, which leverages the mixture-of-experts (MoE) paradigm to transform such spatial–spectral cues into content-adaptive prompts. These prompts are then injected into RGB-pretrained tracking representations to enhance their discriminative capacity. Within MDME, we design a routed-and-shared expert scheme, where multiple directional spatial Mamba streams are sparsely activated and adaptively weighted based on the current spatial patterns, while bidirectional spectral scanning branches are shared to aggregate group-wise spectral features along the wavelength dimension. Furthermore, a temporal memory Mamba (TMM) mechanism is introduced to bridge deep intraframe representations with long-range interframe target evolution. By adopting a unidirectional causal scanning strategy, TMM generates memory-guided temporal modulation signals that continuously refine target descriptors under persistent appearance variations. Extensive experiments on eight public HS tracking datasets demonstrate that HOTMM achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) tracking performance. The code will be available at https://github.com/creenciaxz/HOTMM
author: Junnan Xu, Shaohui Mei, Yiqing Zhou, Yi Wang, Mingyang Ma, Jiao Shi

title: WIMNet: A Whole-Image-Based Mamba Network for Joint Hyperspectral and LiDAR Classification
content: Although recent advances have improved joint hyperspectral images (HSIs) and light detection and ranging (LiDAR) data classification, most existing approaches adopt a patch-based processing strategy that leads to substantial computational redundancy for high-resolution data. Moreover, existing architectures usually process spectral and spatial features independently, making it difficult to capture their relationships and thus limiting the fusion performance. To address the above challenges, we propose a whole-image-based Mamba network (WIMNet) that performs image-level fusion for joint hyperspectral and LiDAR classification. Unlike existing patch-based approaches, the proposed WIMNet consists of two dedicated branches: a joint spectral–spatial Mamba branch (JSMB), incorporating a spectral-spatial mamba block (SSMB) with a spectral modulation mechanism (SMM) for adaptive spectral–spatial joint learning, and the multiscale LiDAR Mamba branch (MLMB) for LiDAR feature extraction. Then their features are integrated via a multimodal feature fusion module (MFFM) to produce the final classification map. Final extensive experiments on Houston2013, MUUFL, and Trento datasets demonstrate that WIMNet achieves state-of-the-art accuracy while reducing GFLOPs and inference time by up to 99% compared with existing 11 leading methods, confirming its remarkable efficiency in joint hyperspectral and LiDAR classification. Code will be available at https://github.com/Dufu9/WIMNet
author: Jiangyun Li, Jiaheng Han, Hao Wang, Xiaochen Zhang, Peixian Zhuang

title: Large-Scale Monitoring of Soil Organic Matter and Salinization Using Satellite Hyperspectral Imagery and a Deep Residual Attention Network
content: Soil degradation threatens environmental health and sustainable land use, with declining soil organic matter (SOM) and increasing salinization being two key manifestations. Accurate assessment of the spatial distribution of SOM and salinity is of great importance for both agricultural productivity and ecological conservation. This article proposes a novel end-to-end deep-learning model—the residual attention regression network (RARNet)—to enable quantitative estimation of soil parameters based on satellite-borne hyperspectral imagery. RARNet combines 2-D spectral convolution, residual feature learning, and attention mechanisms to jointly capture local spectral patterns and global spectral dependencies, thereby improving spatial–spectral feature representation. The experimental results obtained over a 17 000 km2 black-soil region using a satellite hyperspectral dataset show the improved predictive performance of RARNet. Under the fivefold cross-validation setting, the validation coefficients of determination (R2) are 0.7291 and 0.8368, the root-mean-square error (RMSE) values are 2.0986 and 2.4806, and the mean absolute error (MAE) values are 1.7449 and 2.0152 for SOM and soil salinity, respectively. Compared with representative deep learning baselines, including long short-term memory (LSTM) and convolutional neural network (CNN) with spectral attention, RARNet achieved higher average performance. It also improved R2 by approximately 10%–15% compared with the ResNet backbone and by 15%–25% compared with the CARS-XGBoost method. The trained model was applied to the study area, generating large-scale spatial distribution maps of SOM and salinity. The mapping results revealed that the region suffers from low SOM levels and widespread salinization, with over half the area showing a salt content of 1.0 g/kg or higher. Overall, the proposed RARNet provides a feasible framework for large-scale mapping of SOM, salinization, and their spatial patterns in the study area. This demonstrates the potential of combining satellite hyperspectral imagery with deep learning for the monitoring of soil properties and land degradation. Although further validation is required for other soil types and climates, the current findings provide a quantitative reference for land restoration and regional ecological risk assessments.
author: Chao Niu, Libin Zhu, Kun Tan, Xue Wang, Run Niu, Xiaojun Xu

title: SAMamba: State Attention Mamba for Hyperspectral Image Classification
content: State-space models (SSMs), such as Mamba, have exhibited superior performance in long-range dependency modeling with linear complexity. However, their causal modeling paradigm, topology-disruptive serialization, and channel-independent state evolution limit their ability to model noncausal spectral–spatial dependencies in hyperspectral images (HSIs). To address these limitations, this article proposes state attention Mamba (SAMamba), a dual-branch heterogeneous state-space framework for HSI classification. Unlike existing Mamba-based methods that mainly rely on predefined scanning strategies or feature-level fusion, SAMamba explicitly intervenes in two key stages of state-space modeling: sequence construction and state evolution. In the spatial branch, the amplitude–phase cooperative semantic sequence construction (APC) module constructs semantic sequences to enhance long-range interactions among semantically correlated pixels, while the frequency-aware state-space augmentation (FAS) module compensates for the spatial structural bias introduced by semantic reordering by injecting multiscale structural priors into hidden states. In the spectral branch, the bidirectional spectral sequence construction (BSS) module constructs forward and backward spectral sequences for bidirectional spectral context modeling, while the bidirectional fusion spatial-aware state (BFS) module fuses bidirectional spectral context and spatial information in the state space to strengthen spectral–spatial joint representation. Experimental results on four public HSI benchmarks verify the effectiveness of SAMamba. Specifically, on the Indian Pines dataset, SAMamba improves over the second-best method by 0.46%, 1.56%, and 0.52% in overall accuracy (OA), average accuracy (AA), and Kappa coefficient, respectively.
author: Shuyu Ye, Bing Tu, Bo Liu, Yan He, Lianhui Liang, Peng Wang, Jun Li

title: Hyperspectral Anomaly Detection and Unmixing via Joint Optimization and Deep Interactive Network
content: Hyperspectral unmixing (HU) and hyperspectral anomaly detection (HAD) are two momentous tasks in remote sensing and Earth observation, where HU identifies material composition and abundance and HAD locates rare targets against complex backgrounds. Existing approaches predominantly follow a sequential unmixing-then-detection paradigm, in which detection performance is inherently constrained by the fidelity and stability of the preceding unmixing stage, leading to suboptimal detection synergy. To address this limitation, we propose a joint optimization and deep interactive network (JODINet) that unifies HU and HAD within a single end-to-end framework. Departing from cascade architectures, the JODINet employs a weight-sharing encoder followed by a parallel background decoder for anomaly discrimination and a spectral decoder for abundance estimation. Central to our design is an abundance-background interaction module (ABIM) that explicitly models the latent dependency between abundance maps and background reconstruction, enabling bidirectional refinement across both branches. Furthermore, a learnable endmember learning module (ELM) is incorporated to precisely estimate background spectral signatures, mitigating the instability common in unsupervised unmixing. By jointly optimizing both tasks, the proposed framework establishes a mutual feedback mechanism that improves background estimation and enhances detection performance, while anomaly-driven cues guide more accurate spectral decomposition. Extensive experiments on real-world HAD and HU datasets demonstrate that the proposed JODINet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in both anomaly detection accuracy and unmixing precision.
author: Xu He, Yingqian Wang, Qiang Ling, Kun Li, Yihang Luo, Shilin Zhou

title: YOLO-UOD: Location-Edge Co-Perception Network With Multiscale Features Fusion for Underwater Object Detection
content: Underwater object detection (UOD) plays a vital role in marine resource exploration and ecological conservation. However, underwater environments pose significant challenges, including nonuniform light attenuation, color distortion between marine organisms and backgrounds, and severe image degradation. These factors lead to blurred boundaries, texture loss, and difficulties in distinguishing objects from their surrounding backgrounds, particularly for small, occluded, and camouflaged objects. To address these issues, we propose a location-edge co-perception network with multiscale feature fusion (YOLO-UOD) for UOD. First, we design a wavelet-cross-stage partial (CSP) synergistic feature enhancement module (WCS-M) that guides CSP operations with discrete wavelet transform (DWT) to enhance feature representation, effectively distinguishing foreground from background. Second, we propose a location perception module (LPM) and an edge perception module (EPM) to enhance semantic preservation and edge-texture awareness using deformable convolutions (DConvs) and adaptive attention mechanisms. Furthermore, a partial bidirectional spatial attention (PBSA) Module is proposed to enhance target localization and recognition performance while effectively suppressing high-frequency noise. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets (DUO, RUOD, and UTDAC2020) demonstrate that the proposed method effectively alleviates issues such as blurred boundaries and texture loss in UOD. The key modules also significantly improve performance compared to existing YOLO-series methods, showcasing strong generalization ability. The model code is available at https://github.com/liuyuanjie123-tech/YOLO-UOD.
author: Fulin Luo, Yuanjie Liu, Tan Guo, Chuan Fu, Yukun Lin, Tao Xiang, Bo Du

title: Wavelet-Enhanced Adaptive Representation Learning for Deep-Sea Manganese Nodule Image Segmentation
content: Deep-sea manganese nodules constitute a key seabed metal resource, and accurate segmentation of their spatial distribution in near-bottom imagery is critical for supporting autonomous mining-vehicle operations, path planning, and resource evaluation. However, achieving accurate segmentation remains challenging due to low contrast, complex backgrounds, and significant scale variation, and existing models still struggle to capture fine details and robust multi-scale cues. To address these issues, we present the WEAR-Seg model, which establishes a unified spatial–frequency representation framework that enhances boundaries, textures, and multi-scale structures in complex underwater imagery. The model integrates three key components: 1) WaveFuse, which uses wavelet decomposition and reconstruction to fuse frequency cues with spatial context to enhance structural details; 2) AdaRep, which adaptively captures multi-scale features to highlight key regions and suppress background noise; and 3) LiteConv, a lightweight convolutional module that improves small-object recognition and model deployability. We construct a dedicated manganese nodule dataset from Jiaolong submersible sea-trial imagery to support training and evaluation. Experimental results show that WEAR-Seg outperforms representative baselines across standard metrics. Ablation studies confirm that WaveFuse and AdaRep contribute most, especially for small targets and complex backgrounds, demonstrating the model’s effectiveness in accurate and fine-detail segmentation under challenging underwater conditions.
author: Yugang Ren, Guanhong Zhai, Yunsai Chen, Shengli Wang, Xinguang Du, Zhiyuan Meng

title: Frequency–Phase Aware Mamba Fusion for Multiscale Spatial Representation in Remote Sensing Image Interpretation
content: Remote sensing images (RSIs) exhibit complex spatial patterns due to diverse object scales, orientations, and distributions. These characteristics pose significant challenges for accurate interpretation. While existing methods focus on spatial feature extraction or rotational constraints, they often overlook the intrinsic relationship between frequency–phase information and spatial structures, which is critical for precise localization and classification. In this article, we propose a generic and flexible feature fusion framework, called phase-aware Mamba fusion mechanism (PMFM), which jointly captures frequency–phase patterns and global spatial dependencies. PMFM consists of two core modules, i.e., frequency–phase position encoding (PhasePE), which enhances positional encoding by embedding frequency–phase cues, and cross-attention–Mamba progressive fusion (CaMPF), which integrates global–local information across multiscale feature maps using cross-attention and vision Mamba. Furthermore, we design a PMFM-based feature pyramid architecture (PMFM-FPN) via a cross-scale concatenation strategy, enabling seamless integration into various detection and segmentation frameworks. Extensive experiments on five public RSI benchmarks (DOTA, DIOR-R, SISP, ISPRS Potsdam, and Vaihingen) across three major tasks, i.e., horizontal detection, oriented detection, and semantic segmentation, demonstrate that PMFM consistently improves performance and achieves competitive accuracy and robustness. In addition, an auxiliary experiment on ExDark further explores the behavior of phase-aware representations under general low-light visual conditions.
author: Xinyue Liu, Hongning Liu, Mingjie Xie, Dongli Xu, Xianchao Zhang, Pengming Feng, Jian Guan

title: Effective Reinforcement Learning for Remote Sensing Vision-Language Models via Supervised Knowledge Injection
content: While vision-language models (VLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities in general-domain visual understanding, their performance in remote sensing (RS) tasks remains limited due to domain discrepancies and the scarcity of RS data in typical vision-language pretraining corpora. Recent advances show that reinforcement learning (RL) can enhance VLMs’ reasoning capabilities by guiding models to perform intermediate “thought” steps before answering. However, applying RL directly to RS scenarios is often ineffective, as VLMs lack fundamental domain knowledge to begin with. To address this, we propose a novel paradigm that first equips VLMs with RS knowledge in the RS visual grounding task through supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on a curated dataset of chain-of-thought (CoT) samples specific to RS. We then conduct RL to further strengthen the learned capabilities. Experiments on the in-domain dataset show that our RL-enhanced model outperforms its SFT-only counterpart. Furthermore, evaluations on the out-of-domain dataset demonstrate that our method fosters better generalization in RS reasoning. Our approach offers a promising direction for adapting general-purpose VLMs to domain-specific visual understanding tasks.
author: Zilun Zhang, Peng Liu, Haozhan Shen, Tiancheng Zhao, Zhaojun Liu, Jianwei Yin

title: UPchange: Unpaired Supervised Semantic Change Detection for High-Resolution Remote Sensing Imagery
content: High-resolution remote sensing imagery semantic change detection (SCD) identifies both the regions of change and land cover variations in multitemporal data, which has huge application potential in many fields. However, most of the existing methods rely on bitemporal remote sensing images with paired change and semantic labels, which are costly and labor-intensive to acquire. In this article, we propose a novel framework named UPchange, which enables training of a semantic change detector using single-temporal labeled data. First, we generate a substantial volume of supervised semantic change labels on unpaired data by treating different semantic annotations in these data as indicators of change. Furthermore, due to the imbalance between generated positive change samples and negative unchanged samples, a new formulation named ChangeType was proposed to transform the SCD problem into a classification problem, in which each type of change is assigned a specific class, thus splitting the dominant binary change samples into different semantic change samples. Lastly, to bridge the domain gap between unpaired data with pseudo-labels in the training process and paired data in the inference process, a temporal change adaption (TCA) was used, with unpaired data as the source domain and paired data as the target domain. We conducted extensive experiments on two open-source SCD datasets (Hi-UCD mini and SECOND). The proposed UPchange framework outperforms two benchmarks under single-temporal settings and can achieve superior performance under bitemporal settings, demonstrating the efficiency and superiority of the method. Moreover, we deployed the model on three large-scale regions for semantic change inference, demonstrating its generalizability and robustness in real-world applications. Code is available at:https://github.com/xian0706/UPchange
author: Jingxian Yang, Ailong Ma, Shiqi Tian, Zhuo Zheng, Wei Wu, Yanfei Zhong

title: SFSC-CDNet: A Novel Change Detection Approach for Collaborative Inference of Structure and Semantics in Multitemporal Remote Sensing Images
content: Semantic change detection (SCD) aims to simultaneously identify change locations and semantic transfer relationships in multitemporal remote sensing images. However, existing difference modeling predominantly emphasizes spatial similarity, often struggling to maintain stability in cross-temporal difference representation while ensuring high-fidelity boundary delineation. This article introduces an end-to-end unified framework, SFSC-CDNet, which facilitates collaborative inference of structure and semantics through spatial–frequency synergistic difference interaction (SFSDI), edge-guided interactive semantic transmission (EGIST), and the bidirectional semantic consistency-guided multitask decoder (SCCM). It incorporates a cross-temporal semantic consistency loss  ${\mathcal {L}}_{\mathrm {cwsc}}$ , explicitly constraining the stability of bidirectional semantic mapping, thereby enhancing the model’s robustness and boundary fidelity. Experiments conducted on four SCD datasets (SECOND, LevirSCD, Landsat-SCD, WUSU) demonstrate that this method significantly surpasses the current state-of-the-art (SOTA), achieving SeK improvements of 0.87%, 3.88%, 1.68%, and 6.73%, respectively. Boundary-aware evaluation results indicate that SFSC-CDNet consistently performs well in terms of  $\mathrm {F}_{boundary}$  and Brier score, reflecting its superior boundary delineation capability and more reliable probability calibration. In addition, under comparable parameter conditions, it achieves a reduction in GFLOPs by 72.51%–74.56% compared to the SOTA method, significantly lowering computational costs and confirming that the method establishes a better accuracy-efficiency balance in both bi-temporal and multitemporal inference scenarios. The associated code is available at https://github.com/Brookyway/SFSC-CDNet
author: Shu Qian, Qian Shen, Junli Li, Xin Zhang, Rong Bu, Changming Zhu

title: GeoAttX: A Novel Framework for Cloud Image Forecasting Using Geostationary Satellite Observations
content: Cloud imagery provides critical parameters—including temperature, precipitation, and cloud properties—that are valuable for scientific research. Real-time cloud image forecasting could greatly extend the utility of such data for practical applications, yet large-scale cloud extrapolation and prediction remain challenging due to limitations in accuracy, high computational costs, and cumulative errors. To address these challenges, we introduce GeoAttX, a geographic attention network for diverse tasks, is proposed, a novel deep learning framework that integrates geographic attention mechanisms to forecast cloud movement using data from the FengYun-4B (FY-4B) geostationary meteorological satellite, which updates every 15 min. The framework employs two core algorithms: a reversed greedy algorithm (RGA), which reduces the number of autoregressive steps by reversing the order of the standard greedy approach, and secondary-order standardization (SoS), which renormalizes pixel distributions to maintain consistent brightness statistics across inputs, mitigating error accumulation and improving accuracy. GeoAttX demonstrates strong performance in cloud dynamics prediction, achieving R2 values of 0.9938, 0.9539, and 0.7489 for lead times of 15, 60, and 180 min, respectively. These results represent a substantial advance in satellite-based cloud image forecasting via deep learning, offering valuable insights for atmospheric modeling and disaster management. The source code is available at https://github.com/songzijiang/GeoAttX
author: Zijiang Song, Lina Yuan, Ting Liu, Yuying Li, Zhixiang Cheng, Ya Wang, Xi Chen, Min Liu

title: Multitarget Segmentation and Adaptive Tracking of Single-Photon LiDAR in Ultralow-SNR UAV Swarm Scene
content: Single-photon LiDAR (SPL) offers extreme detection sensitivity for long-range sensing but remains fundamentally challenged in multitarget scenarios where echoes from different objects strongly overlap under severe photon scarcity. This limitation prevents reliable perception of unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) swarms in low-SNR environments. Here, we present a multitarget residual-enhanced adaptive tracking (MRAT) framework that resolves overlapping photon echoes by exploiting statistical differences in photon-event distributions across time-of-flight and frame domains. By modeling residual structures in photon-event histograms and enforcing spatiotemporal consistency, MRAT separates and tracks multiple UAV targets even under severe spatial overlap. Field experiments using a Geiger-mode APD (Gm-APD) SPL prototype demonstrate reliable segmentation and trajectory reconstruction of multiple UAVs at ranges up to 1 km across diverse weather conditions and heterogeneous backgrounds (sky, forest, and urban buildings). The system maintains multitarget separability at signal-to-noise ratios down to −14.0814 dB with fewer than 0.1744 average signal photons per pixel and achieves a minimum radial resolution of 4.5 m while sustaining subpixel tracking accuracy during severe target interactions and occlusions. These results provide the first experimental demonstration of SPL for UAV-swarm tracking and suggest a pathway toward long-range multitarget perception for wide-area low-altitude monitoring.
author: Dongfang Guo, Jianfeng Sun, Xin Zhou, Sheng Chen, Yinbo Zhang, Sining Li, Yanchen Qu

title: Integrating Earth Observation and Digital Tools for Agricultural Mapping and Monitoring: The Mozambique Case Study
content: Accurate and timely agricultural data are vital in low- and middle-income countries for effective national planning, informed food policy decisions, and progress toward achieving sustainable development goal (SDG) 2 (zero hunger). Annual field surveys are costly, time-consuming, hampered by weak infrastructure, and vulnerable to extreme weather. This article presents the architecture developed in the statistics from space project, which integrates Earth observation (EO) data, digital tools for field-data collection, and a cloud-based aggregation and visualization platform to propose a cost-effective solution for efficient agricultural monitoring. The workflow was tested in Mozambique and includes: first, an EO-based sampling-frame methodology for heterogeneous agro-landscapes, second, an open-source smartphone application for efficient, geo-referenced field data collection, third, a data-aggregation and visualization system for real-time quality monitoring and national-level dissemination, and fourth, automated methods for EO-based crop type mapping. The project delivered a comprehensive framework for integrating EO and digital technologies into efficient and scalable workflows. High-resolution crop type maps (openly accessible) and statistics were generated for the main crops in three pilot provinces, Gaza, Manica, and Zambezia, for the 2024/25 season.
author: Claudia Paris, Rolf A. de By, Robert Ohuru, Mehmet Furkan Çelik, Florian J. Ellsäßer, Gabriel Tseng, Yawen Zhang, Sosdito Estevão Mananze, Lourenço Manuel, Jawoo Koo, Andrew Nelson

title: LSACT: A Lightweight Compact Transformer With Spatial Attention for Complex Remote Sensing Scene Classification
content: Remote sensing scene classification remains a challenging task due to large variations in scale, viewpoint, texture, and the limited availability of annotated data. To address these challenges, this work presents the Lightweight Spatial Attention Compact Transformer (LSACT), a model that combines convolutional inductive biases with spatial attention to achieve efficient and accurate representation learning. The architecture integrates a compact tokenization stem, a bias positional embedding, and a hierarchical CT block composed of a Local Pattern Unit, a Decoupled Fully Connected Attention Block, and a Deep Inverted Residual Feed Forward Network, which together enhance spatial coherence and strengthen both local and global feature modeling. Experiments on four widely used benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed design, achieving 94.24% overall accuracy on EuroSAT, 77.69% on AID, 77.98% on UCMerced, and 74.88% on NWPU. The ablation study confirms the contribution of each component, with the attention module providing the largest improvement, while t-SNE visualizations show compact and well separated clusters that reflect strong discriminative feature learning. These findings indicate that LSACT offers a balanced solution that combines accuracy with computational efficiency, making it suitable for practical remote sensing scene classification tasks.
author: Mohammed Q. Alkhatib, Ali Jamali

title: Lightweight SAR: A 1.5-Bit Strategy
content: Nowadays lightweight radar has become an important technique in autonomous driving systems for its all-weather sensing ability. Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imaging based on one-bit sampling is able to considerably simplify system deployment and cut down manufacturing costs. However, a high oversampling rate is usually required to avoid the performance degradation resulting from aliasing of one-bit quantization noise. This will counteract the storage saving advantages brought by low quantization level. In this article, to facilitate its applicability on vehicle platforms, a lightweight strategy of 1.5-bit SAR imaging is devised to inherit the merit of one-bit quantization in simplification but circumvent the requirement of high sampling rate. Compared with one-bit quantization, only additional 0.5 bit per sample is introduced to alleviate the awkward harmonic problem due to the nonlinear effect of quantization. In this way, the quality of SAR imagery can be significantly improved. Besides, the enhanced performance of the proposed method does not rely on the sampling rate. This will benefit the whole procedure of imaging and lighten the system burden and cost consequently. Theoretical analysis and experimental results from a real vehicular SAR system showcase the superiority of our 1.5-bit quantization approach.
author: Shiqi Liu, Bing Li, Bo Zhao, Lei Huang, Hing Cheung So

title: EU-Transformer: An Enhanced U-Shape Transformer via Texture Retention for SAR Image Denoising
content: Synthetic aperture radar (SAR) inherently suffers from speckle noise attributable to its distinct imaging mechanism, imposing significant challenges on the extraction of valid information from SAR images. Consequently, speckle suppression is essential in the processing of SAR images. Current deep learning-based SAR image denoising algorithms struggle to achieve an optimal balance between speckle reduction and texture retention. Regarding the above issue, this article proposes EU-Transformer, an enhanced U-shape Transformer via texture retention for SAR image denoising. Specifically, during the encoding phase, the EU-Transformer employs a multiscale enhanced Transformer block (METB) to extract hierarchical features from the noisy image, thereby capturing multiscale representations. Subsequently, a bottleneck layer is utilized to further distill deep features. In the decoding phase, to fully exploit both local and global image information, this article constructs a dual-branched enhanced Transformer block to progressively reconstruct deep features. Furthermore, to better preserve image textures, a texture retention block is proposed to enhance feature transmission during the skip-connection stage. Finally, the EU-Transformer generates the denoised output through global residual learning. Experimental findings demonstrate that the proposed EU-Transformer outperforms current top-performing denoising algorithms in both quantitative and qualitative analyses.
author: Shuaiqi Liu, Shengye Wang, Yonglu Zhou, Qi Hu, Bing Li, Yudong Zhang

title: Referring Remote Sensing Image Segmentation With Positive-Incentive Visual Adaptation and Logical Reasoning
content: Referring remote sensing image segmentation (RRSIS) aims to segment target regions in aerial imagery according to natural language descriptions. Despite recent advances in vision-language foundation models, their application to RRSIS remains challenging due to severe domain shifts, cluttered backgrounds, and complex spatial layouts. Existing methods mainly suffer from two limitations: insufficient cross-modal alignment caused by natural-image training bias, and limited reasoning ability when handling complex expressions involving multiple entities and spatial constraints. To address these issues, we propose a reasoning-enhanced decoupled framework for RRSIS with two task-oriented modules: a Positive-Incentive Cross-modal Alignment Module (PICAM) and a Referring Logic Reasoning Module (RLRM). PICAM introduces beneficial input-conditional noise into the visual representation, while using textual cues as conditioning signals, thereby alleviating the domain gap between remote sensing imagery and natural-image-pretrained vision-language models and improving the robustness and discriminability of multimodal features. Built upon the alignment-enhanced features, RLRM explicitly introduces large-model reasoning into the localization stage by decomposing referring expressions into target semantics, attributes, anchor objects, and spatial relations, and converting the reasoning process into reliable dense and sparse prompts for downstream segmentation. Based on these two modules, our framework follows a clear decoupled paradigm: alignment, reasoning-guided localization, and prompt-based mask generation. This design enables the model to resolve semantic ambiguity before precise boundary delineation, leading to improved interpretability and better adaptation to complex remote sensing scenarios.
author: Kun Wang, Wei Li, Xiaopeng Liu, Duping Huang, Lei Liu

title: Evaluating SWOT-Derived Surface Quasi-Geostrophic Currents With HF Radar Observations
content: High-resolution sea surface height from the surface water and ocean topography (SWOT) mission provide a unique opportunity to investigate submesoscale ocean dynamics, yet the accuracy of surface current reconstructions from real SWOT measurements remains uncertain. Here, we apply the effective surface quasi-geostrophic (eSQG) method to reconstruct near-surface currents from five SWOT overpasses in the Gulf Stream region and compare the results against coincident high frequency radar observations. The reconstructed velocities show strong correlations with radar currents (R $\geq$ 0.70) and successfully reproduce major flow patterns, including anticyclonic eddies in three cases. The estimated Rossby numbers (0.37–0.59, radar-derived: 0.19–0.29 from radar) confirm SWOT's ability to resolve submesoscale processes, although relative vorticity correlations are lower (R = 0.18–0.47). These weaker correlations arise from SWOT swath-edge errors, eSQG wind sensitivity, representation mismatches, and tidal effects.
